questions.question: What is skill composition?
options: Writing skills in multiple programming languages
Combining multiple skills through layering, chaining, or referencing to build complex workflows
Compressing skills into a single file for performance
Automatically generating skills from code analysis
questions.explanation: Skill composition means combining multiple skills through layering (base + specialized), chaining (output feeds into next skill), or referencing (one skill invokes another) to build complex workflows.

questions.question: How does the Skill tool support parameterized skills?
options: Through a special parameter config file
By requiring all parameters to be defined in settings.json
Through the optional 'args' parameter that passes arguments to the skill
Parameters are not supported — skills always behave the same way
questions.explanation: The Skill tool accepts an optional 'args' parameter. When a user types '/deploy staging', Claude passes 'staging' as the args value, allowing the skill to adapt its behavior.

questions.question: What should you do when two skills have overlapping instructions?
options: Keep both — Claude will figure out which one to follow
Delete one of the skills entirely
Consolidate the overlap into one authoritative skill or extract shared logic into a base skill
Add a priority number to each skill
questions.explanation: Overlapping instructions cause conflicts. The solution is to consolidate into one authoritative skill or extract the shared logic into a base skill that both can build upon.

questions.question: When designing a skill ecosystem, what is the recommended approach?
options: Create one large skill that handles everything
Map team workflows to skills systematically, organized by category
Only create skills for tasks that take more than an hour
Copy skills from other projects without modification
questions.explanation: A well-designed skill ecosystem maps team workflows to skills systematically, organizing them into categories like development, operations, content, and project management.

questions.question: Why should parameterized skills always define default behavior?
options: Because the Skill tool crashes without defaults
So the skill works predictably when no arguments are provided
Because defaults are required by the SKILL.md schema
To make the SKILL.md file longer
questions.explanation: Default behavior ensures the skill works predictably when users invoke it without arguments. For example, a deploy skill might default to production if no environment is specified.

questions.question: In the CertFarmer case study, why does the project use three separate skills instead of one?
options: Because Claude can only load one skill at a time
To keep each skill focused on one domain — content, architecture, and roadmap
Because the project is too large for a single skill
Because each skill is written by a different team member
questions.explanation: CertFarmer uses three focused skills (content-authoring, architecture, project-roadmap) rather than one mega-skill. Each handles its domain, and keeping them separate makes them easier to maintain and update.
